coredata.dc:title: Upconversion photoluminescent metal ion sensors via two photon absorption in graphene oxide quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ha, Hyun Dong
Jang, Min-Ho
Liu, Fei
Cho, Yong-Hoon
Seo, Tae Seok
coredata.dc:description: In-depth study on upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) was presented. Uniform GOQDs with a single-layer and a circular shape within 4nm diameter were fabricated by acidic exfoliation from the graphite nanoparticles. The green UCPL of the GOQDs at ∼563nm was observed by a femto-second (fs) Ti:sapphire pulsed laser (λ ex: 800nm), and the mechanism of the observed UCPL was systemically characterized. We further utilized the UCPL of GOQD as an optical biosensor for detecting aptamer-based metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity, and verified that the fluorescence quenching of the UCPL GOQDs was mediated by the electron transfer between the GOQD and the captured metal ion rather than fluorescence energy transfer. The understanding of the UCPL of graphene quantum dots would enhance their applicability in the biosensing and bioimaging research fields.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigating the UCPL of the GOQDs
Cu2+ detection via UCPL of the GOQDs
UCPL quenching mechanism of the GOQDs by Cu2+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from the Converging Technology Project funded by the Korean Ministry of Environment ( M112-00061-0002-0 ) of Korea, by the Engineering Research Center of Excellence Program of Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (MSIP)/ National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) (Grant NRF-2014-009799 ), and by the GRC project of KI for the NanoCentury. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image and the corresponding FFT pattern of the GOQDs. (b) Size distribution of the GOQDs. (c) AFM image of the GOQDs deposited on mica substrates. Inset: the height profile along the line A-B. (d) Height distribution of the GOQDs. (e) High-resolution XPS C1s spectra of the GOQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL (λ ex =325nm) and PLE (λ de =525nm) spectra of the GOQDs. (b) The maximum peak wavelength of the PL spectra with variation of the excitation wavelength from 260nm to 520nm (black). Inset: photostability analysis of the GOQDs for 1month (1, 5, 10, 11, 13, 15, 17, 25, and 30days). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The PL spectra excited by the 400-nm second-harmonic fs- (blue lines) and by an 800-nmfs-(red lines) Ti:sapphire pulsed laser. (b) The normal PL (blue circles) and UCPL (red circles) intensities versus the excitation power (plotted in a logarithmic scale). The normal PL and UCPL were excited with 405nm (continuous-wave laser) and with 800nm (fs Ti:sapphire laser) wavelength light source, respectively. Note that although the normal PL occurs in all regions of the passing laser beam in the cuvette, while the UCPL takes place only in the narrow and intensified focal plane area. (c) Normalized PL decay time profiles for normal PL (blue) and UCPL via two-photon absorption (red). Inset: enlarged normalized PL decay time profile from 1.5ns to 3.5ns. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The UCPL spectra of the GOQDs depending on the concentration of Cu2+ ion under the excitation at 800nm. (b) Relative quenching efficiency (rQE,%) versus Cu2+ concentration. Inset: photograph images showing the quenching of the UCPL GOQD-aptamer in the presence of Cu2+. (c) A linear plot of I 0/ΔI versus 1/[Cu2+] according to the modified Stern–Volmer equation. (d) Selectivity test for sensing Cu2+ ions by the GOQD-aptamer sensors (red bar). The used metal ion concentration was 50μM. The blue bar represents the rQE of the GOQDs without aptamer in the presence of Cu2+ as a control. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 10mM Cu2+ ions (red line) and the PL spectrum of the GOQDs excited by an 800nm wavelength (green line). (b) PL lifetime of the GOQDs depending on the Cu2+ concentration. The lifetime was obtained when the intensity decreases to 1/e of the maximum value. Inset: A representative PL decay curve of a pristine GOQD-aptamer solution (black) versus a GOQD-aptamer solution with 1mM Cu2+ ions (red). (c) GOQD quenching modeling via electron transfer from the UCPL GOQDs to the Cu2+ ions. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Upconversion photoluminescent metal ion sensors via two photon absorption in graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering (BK21 program) and Institute for the BioCentury, KAIST, 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering (BK21 program) and Institute for the BioCentury

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Graphene Research Center of KI for the NanoCentury, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Graphene Research Center of KI for the NanoCentury
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In-depth study on upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) was presented. Uniform GOQDs with a single-layer and a circular shape within 4nm diameter were fabricated by acidic exfoliation from the graphite nanoparticles. The green UCPL of the GOQDs at ∼563nm was observed by a femto-second (fs) Ti:sapphire pulsed laser (λ ex: 800nm), and the mechanism of the observed UCPL was systemically characterized. We further utilized the UCPL of GOQD as an optical biosensor for detecting aptamer-based metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity, and verified that the fluorescence quenching of the UCPL GOQDs was mediated by the electron transfer between the GOQD and the captured metal ion rather than fluorescence energy transfer. The understanding of the UCPL of graphene quantum dots would enhance their applicability in the biosensing and bioimaging research fields.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique structure of graphene, which is composed of a one-atom-thick two-dimensional crystal of sp2 carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice, contributes to its extraordinary physical and chemical properties, such as high intrinsic mobility, thermal stability, and electrical conductivity [1,2]. Recent efforts to modify the graphene structure, including size, defects or type of dopant added, and functionalization at the edges turn the graphene properties from metallic to semiconductor by tuning the band-gap, expanding its application areas into the nanophotonics and nanoelectronics [3–5]. In particular, a control of the size of graphene to less than 10nm or sp2 nanodomain formation in the sp3 matrix through a reduction process of graphene oxide endows graphene with distinct photoluminescence (PL) properties due to the quantum confinement effect or zig-zag edge effect [6–8]. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) and their derivatives [graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD) and reduced graphene quantum dots (rGOQD)] have been presented, and their PL features are well characterized [9].

GQDs and its derivatives have advantages over conventional fluorescent dyes and semiconducting QDs in terms of cost-effectiveness for their synthesis, low toxicity and reduced photobleaching. Due to these characteristics as well as their good solubility, high biocompatibility, and a broad range of optical absorptivity, GQDs have garnered considerable attention as a novel light-emitting nanomaterial, and they have great potentials to be used as a fluorescent tag for biomolecular sensing and cellular bioimaging applications [10–14]. Moreover, they have revealed intriguing luminescent properties, such as pH-dependent and excitation-dependent PL behaviors [15]. To date, the PL signal of GQDs and its derivatives were shown to be emitted upon irradiation by one-photon excitation using either UV or a blue light source with higher energy than the band-gap of them. A high-energy light source inevitably imposes limitations on the use of GQDs and GOQDs as fluorescent tags for sensing DNA and living cells, as intense UV light can cause photodamage in biomolecules [16,17]. To address this issue, upconversion PL (UCPL) with near-IR excitation is normally adopted for biomolecular imaging, taking advantage of minor autofluorescence background signals, deep tissue penetration depths, and low photodamage levels [18,19]. The UCPL is a phenomenon that the photon energy of emission is higher than the excitation one, and such a phenomenon has been widely reported with solid-state materials doped with rare-earth elements, semiconductors with heterostructure, quantum wells, and quantum dots [20–22]. In the case of the GQD and its derivatives, there are a few reports showing the UCPL phenomena by using a monochromatic light of a xenon lamp as an excitation light source. However, there is a controversy as regards the reasons behind the observed luminescence from the GQDs [15,23–27]. Thus, to fully utilize the PL properties of GQDs and its derivatives, it is necessary to exactly understand their UCPL characteristics. Herein, we propose a facile fabrication route for synthesizing uniform GOQDs and clarify its green emission by means of real UCPL via a femto-second (fs) Ti:sapphire pulsed laser. Furthermore, we used the UCPL of GOQDs for aptamer-based Cu2+ ion detection to screen an environmental water pollutant. Because the aptamer which is linked on the GOQD surface plays a major role to capture the target metal ions, the integrity of the aptamer structure is very important during the photoluminescence measurement of the GOQDs. It is well known that the UV irradiation could cause the photodamage of DNA which might decrease the functionality of the aptamer. Using the UCPL property of GOQDs could eliminate such a concern in our study. Lastly, we verified that metal ion detection was mediated by the electron transfer mechanism between the GOQD and the captured metal ion rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

Graphite nanoparticles (GNPs) (Avg. diameter: 3–4nm, 93%) were obtained from SkySpring Nanomaterials (USA). Extra pure H2SO4 (95%) and HNO3 (60–62%) were purchased from JUNSEI (Japan). The amine modified Cu2+ specific aptamer (5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TTC TAA TAC GAT TTA GAA TAA ATC TGG GCC TCT TTT TAA GAA C-3′) was purchased from Bioneer Corporation (Korea). All heavy metal salts, N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHS), and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Deionized (D.I.) water obtained from a Milli-Q water system (Millipore, USA) was used for all aqueous solutions. All chemicals and solvents were used directly without any further purification.

1g of GNPs were pre-oxidized in a concentrated H2SO4 (60mL) and HNO3 (20mL) solution for 3h under mild ultrasonication (100W). Then, the solution went through refluxing for 12h at 100°C. After cooling to room temperature (RT), the mixture was diluted with deionized water. The suspension was centrifuged at 20,000rpm for 20min, and a supernatant was carefully obtained. The supernatant was dialyzed to remove acidic ions and then adjusted to pH 8 by adding a certain amount of a diluted NaOH solution. The solution was further dialyzed to completely remove the salt ions in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1kDa) for 3days. Finally, the GOQDs were obtained in an aqueous solution with bright yellow color and they revealed a strong green PL upon excitation with a 365nm UV lamp. Unlike a well-known Hummers method, our fabrication route for GOQDs doesn’t require a long oxidation time and a strong oxidant such as KMnO4 [9].

To functionalize the GOQD by a Cu2+ specific aptamer, 2.5mL of 20mM EDC and 2.5mL of 5mM NHS were added to 10mL of a 0.01mg/mL GOQD solution. The mixture was stirred with a magnetic bar for 2h. Then, a 20nM aptamer solution was added, and was kept stirring in a dark chamber at RT overnight. Afterwards, the dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off of 50,000Da was used to purify the unconjugated aptamer (the molecular weight of the aptamer: 13189.7Da) to produce the GOQD-aptamer solution. For Cu2+ ion detection, a certain amount of Cu2+ ions were added to the GOQD-aptamer solution, and then, UCPL measurement was carried out by using a fs pulsed Ti:sapphire laser [an excitation wavelength (λ ex)=800nm, Coherent, Chameleon Ultra II] as an excitation light source and a high-sensitive photomultiplier tube as a PL detector. The experiment conditions are as follows: the laser pulse duration is 140fs, the spot size on the focus plane of lens is about 240μm measured by a Knife edge method, the quartz cell volume (L×W×H) is 10mm×10mm×35mm (3.5ml), and the focusing lens of 1inch diameter has 50mm focal length. The selectivity test was performed by adding 50μM Ca2+, Cd2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Hg2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+ under the identical experimental conditions. In order to elucidate the recombination dynamics depending on the concentration of Cu2+, we carried out time-resolved PL experiments. A mode-locked fs pulsed Ti:sapphire laser system was used as an excitation source, and a streak camera (Hamamatsu, C7700-01) was utilized to measure the decay profile of the PL spectra at RT.

Atomic force microscope (AFM, Veeco D3100, USA) images of the GOQDs were obtained under the conditions of relative humidity of 43%, a tapping mode at a 1–3Hz scan rate and a 512×512pixel resolution. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images of the GOQD were analyzed with an ultra-thin carbon film on a holey carbon support film (400mesh) at an acceleration voltage of 300kV. Raman characterization of the GOQDs was conducted by a high resolution dispersive Raman microscope with an Ar ion continuous-wave (cw) laser at 514.5nm (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by a Thermo VG Scientific Sigma Probe system, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted by IR Prestige-21 (Dong-Il SHIMADZU Corp.) over the range from 600 to 4000cm−1. The normal PL measurements were carried out using a 325nm He-Cd cw laser and UV spectrometers (Maya2000, Ocean Optics, USA) at RT. Monochromatic light from a 300W Xenon lamp was dispersed by a monochromator and focused on the samples for recording PL excitation (PLE) and excitation-wavelength dependent PL with low-excitation power (<0.1mW) at RT. In order to measure the absolute QYs accurately, we used an absolute PL quantum yield measurement system (C9920-02G, HAMAMATSU), which estimates total photon flux through an integration sphere. To compare the spectral profiles by one-photon and two-photon excitation, we measured the PL spectra by using the two wavelengths of the fs Ti:sapphire pulse laser [400nm (second-harmonic-generation) and 800nm] and a streak camera detector (Hamamatsu, C7700-01) at RT. The excitation power-dependent normal PL and UCPL were performed by a 405nm cw laser diode and a 800nmfs Ti:sapphire pulsed laser, respectively, and a high-sensitive photomultiplier tube was used as a PL detector. Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, SHIMADZU, Japan). In addition, the test of photostability and UCPL by a monochromatic light via a lamp source was carried out using a PC spectrophotometer (RF-5301, SHIMADZU, Japan). All the optical data of the GOQDs were obtained in the precision cells made of quartz suprasil.

First, we employed spherical GNPs whose diameter was 3–4nm as a starting material to produce homogenous and single-layer GOQDs. HRTEM and AFM analysis of the GNPs show that the GNPs displayed a spherical particle morphology and 3nm height (Fig. S1). Theoretically, 5–7 layers of graphene sheets were stacked to form a single GNP. The GOQDs were synthesized by ultrasonication and a simple acidic chemical exfoliation reaction in a H2SO4/HNO3 mixture at 100°C. After centrifugation under 20,000rpm, dialysis process was followed for 3days for complete removal of ions or residues which can interact with the GOQDs, reducing their PL intensity. The produced GOQDs revealed strong green fluorescence upon excitation with a 365nm UV lamp (Fig. S2). The quantum yields (QYs) of the GOQDs was about 1.2% under the excitation of 325nm. HRTEM, AFM, and XPS data of the as-synthesized GOQDs are presented in Fig. 1 . The HRTEM data and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Fig. 1a) display circular GOQDs with high crystalline structure having a lattice spacing of 2.4Å, which matches the hexagonal pattern of graphene with (1120) lattice fringes [28]. The typical diameter of the GOQDs was less than 4nm, and the size distribution of the produced GOQDs was quite narrow (Fig. 1b).

Fig. 1c and d show an AFM image and the height distribution of the GOQDs deposited on a mica substrate. A statistical analysis of the GOQD height from 150 samples shows that more than 90% of the GOQDs are less than 1nm thickness, suggesting that the generated GOQDs typically have a single layer [9]. The Raman spectrum of the GOQDs exhibits three typical peaks at 1374, 1605 and around 2680cm−1, indicating the D band, the G band and the 2D band of graphene, respectively (Fig. S3). To quantify the oxygenous functional groups on the GOQDs, XPS and FT-IR analyses were undertaken (Fig. 1e and Fig. S4). The XPS data revealed that the GOQDs contain a sp3 carbon peak with a hydroxyl group at 286.05eV (13.78%), a carbonyl group at 287.88eV (18.17%) and a carboxylic acid group at 289.03eV (16.97%), while the sp2 carbon peak mainly appeared at 284.8eV with 51.08%. This result implies that 48.92% carbon on the GOQDs was linked with oxygen functional groups, which could contribute to high water solubility as well as biomolecular conjugation moieties. The FT-IR spectrum of the GOQDs also supports the existence of oxygenous groups by showing a CO stretching vibration peak at 1100cm−1, a CO stretching vibration peak at 1725cm−1 and a OH stretching peak at 3400cm−1 (Fig. S4).

To understand the optical properties of the synthesized GOQDs, we carried out various optical experiments. Fig. 2 a shows the PL spectrum of the GOQDs using an excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 325nm and the PLE spectrum at the detection emission wavelength (λ de) of 525nm. The PL spectrum revealed broad spectral emission dispersion with a maximum peak wavelength at 520nm. The PLE spectrum also shows a broad spectrum with maximum intensity at 325nm. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum gradually increased from 800nm to 200nm, which suggests the existence of band tails caused by the defect states (the red line in Fig. S5) [15,28,29]. We also observed an excitation-dependent PL behavior in which the wavelengths of the PL peak were red-shifted in proportional to the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. S6. For the excitation wavelength of less than 440nm, the PL peak wavelength remained at 520nm, but for the excitation wavelengths which exceeded 440nm, the PL wavelength was red-shifted from 520nm. These optical properties indicate that numerous and complex band-gap states of the GOQDs exist. These were formed by inhomogeneity in terms of size and oxygenous functional groups. In addition, we confirmed the photostability of the GOQDs periodically under 400nm of excitation for one month. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2b, the GOQDs showed not only stable PL intensity but also no aggregation for one month. Furthermore, we also investigated the photostability of the GOQDs against photobleaching by recording the fluorescence intensity with continuous irradiation using a 325nm cw laser (at 20mW and 30mW). Even after continuous irradiation for 450 sec, the emission intensity didn’t show any decrease (Fig. S7). The excellent photostability of the GOQDs can provide strong advantages for the prolonged monitoring of PL probes in biosystems.

Recently, there have been several reports on the UCPL phenomena from GQDs/GOQDs using a monochromatic light dispersed from a lamp source [15,23–27]. However, apparently, the same results can be observed by unintended shorter wavelength excitation at half of the wavelength (λ ex/2) of the set monochromator (λ ex) via the second-order diffraction of the light, which would coincide with the light with λ ex at the same set wavelength of the monochromator. To confirm this type of unwanted effect, we examined the possibility of the apparent UCPL using a long-pass filter (which only transmits longer wavelength than 405nm) in front of the monochromatic light of a xenon lamp via a monochromator. While the PL of the GOQDs (∼496nm) was observed at the set excitation wavelengths of 700nm and 800nm (without the use of the filter), the apparent UCPL disappeared when the long-pass filter was used (Fig. S8). In order to avoid this erroneous excitation situation, we used an fs Ti:sapphire pulsed laser system, with which we could eliminate the contribution of the undesirable shorter wavelength excitation which is derived from the second-order diffraction of the light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the UCPL of the GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with the normal PL which appeared at ∼520nm under the 400nmfs laser excitation (second-harmonic), the UCPL was observed at ∼570nm under the 800nmfs laser excitation, resulting in a red shift of ∼50nm (0.21eV) with respect to the normal PL. In order to understand the origin of UCPL, we carried out excitation power dependent PL experiments. Fig. 3b shows the relationship between the integrated PL intensities, and the excitation power at 405nm (blue circles) and 800nm (red circles). By fitting with straight lines, the normal PL intensity was found to be almost linearly proportional to the excitation power (slope∼0.85), while the UCPL intensity increased quadratically with the excitation power (slope∼2.01). The quadratic dependence of UCPL could be explained by two photon absorption (TPA) or Auger processes [30–37]. In general, the UCPL originated from Auger process occurs in heterostructures consisting of different bandgap materials, since it requires the generation of electron-hole pairs or excitons in a smaller band-gap material by initial excitation and successive generation of higher energy carriers via Auger process [38–41]. However, in case of the GOQDs, the incident photons are not energetic enough to produce electron-hole pairs or excitons. Therefore, the Auger processes may be ruled out as a possible mechanism to explain the observed UCPL. On the other hand, the TPA can be accomplished by the simultaneous absorption of two photons through a real or virtual intermediate state within the band-gap of a material [31,32]. In the real intermediate state, the TPA is occurred by consecutive processes of the first excitation from the ground state to the real intermediate state and the second excitation from the real intermediate state to the final state. So, if the real intermediate state has radiative recombination paths, it can be confirmed through PL experiments via relatively low-average-power cw excitation and the decay lifetime is fairly long (more than nsec) [30]. However, we did not observe the UCPL signal from the GOQDs with a 633-nm excitation of a cw He-Ne laser (Fig. S9a). In addition, under the excitation of 800nm (a fs Ti:sapphire laser), no normal PL signal was seen in the wavelength range between 850nm and 1200nm (Fig. S9b). Hellmann et al. reported that the rise time of barrier UCPL by TPA via real intermediate states in quantum well structure is much longer than that of barrier PL by above-barrier excitation, because the UCPL from barrier occurs for the second excitation step induced by both PL photons of quantum well and laser photons [38]. In our case, the rise time between normal PL and UCPL of GOQDs was not discernible (Fig. 3c). Therefore, even though it is difficult to ignore the existence of a real intermediate state with fast non-radiative decay channels, our experimental results suggest that the TPA via a real intermediate state with radiative recombination paths may seem infeasible in GOQDs. Meanwhile, the TPA via a virtual intermediate state typically occurs at high excitation because the virtual intermediate state has an extremely short decay lifetime (fsec) and needs a large density of photons for the excitation [30]. We observed that as shown in the inset of Fig. 3b, the normal PL (left) was generated over the whole beam-path of the 405-nm cw laser, whereas the UCPL (right) under 800-nm pulse laser excitation was generated only near the focal plane of the lens, the highest photon flux area. For these reasons, we believe that the observed UCPL from GOQDs is primarily due to the TPA via a virtual intermediate state generated by a short-pulsed excitation, although we do not rule out the possibility of the TPA via a real intermediate state with fast non-radiative decay channels. Furthermore, we measured the TPA coefficient under excitation at 800nm. The nonlinear transmissivity of a two-photon absorbing can be described by:
(1)
T (
I
0
, λ ) = 1 / ( 1 + β ( λ )
I
0
L )
where I 0 is the peak intensity of the incident light, L is the path length of the samples, and β(λ) is the two-photon absorption coefficient (in units of cm/GW) [42]. We performed nonlinear transmission measurements at 800nm excitation wavelength with a Ti:sapphire fs laser (80MHz). The laser beam was focused onto 1-cm long aqueous dispersion of GOQDs (∼1mg/mL) in a cuvette. The two-photon absorption coefficient was ∼0.01cm/GW under excitation at 800nm.

Regarding the red shift of the UCPL peak compared to that of a normal PL, similar red-shift behavior has been presented for the colloidal QDs [30–33] and organic dyes [34–37], and it might be attributed to laser heating effect [34,35], self-absorption [36,37], and different excitation cross section of defect states and/or slightly larger particles [30–33] in the up-conversion process. In our case, we believe that the laser heating and self-absorption processes were minimal because the red shift was not observed in other reference samples (i.e., Rhodamine 6G, green CdSe QDs, and red CdSe QDs) under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S10), and the energy of UCPL peak of the GOQDs was almost the same regardless of the excitation power (Fig. S11). It was reported that colloidal semiconductor QDs with defect states or slightly larger size can be preferentially excited by TPA rather than one photon absorption owing to the greater excitation cross-section [30–32,43]. In the same context, the red shift of UCPL observed in the GOQDs can be originated from more defect states and/or size distribution in the sample. To investigate the relationship between the red shift behavior and the oxygen defect states, we prepared rGOQDs with lower degree of defect states by removing the oxygen sites (Fig. S12). Reduced red-shift of ∼0.17eV was found for rGOQDs compared to that of the GOQDs (∼0.21eV), indicating that the degree of the red-shift is strongly associated with the defect states of the GOQDs.

We utilized the UCPL of the GOQDs for an aptamer-mediated Cu2+ biosensing application. Given that DNA does not absorb the excitation wavelength at 800nm, photodamage of the DNA molecules can be minimized and the secondary aptamer structure can be retained. Fig. S13 outlines the strategies of Cu2+ detection using the UCPL GOQD sensors. After obtaining green luminescent GOQDs from the GNPs, amino-modified Cu2+-specific aptamers (5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TTC TAA TAC GAT TTA GAA TAA ATC TGG GCC TCT TTT TAA GAA C-3′) were linked on the GOQDs through an amide bond to produce GOQD-aptamer conjugates. The success of the conjugation with the aptamer was confirmed by observing the 260nm shoulder peak in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (the black line in Fig. S5). When Cu2+ ions were present, the Cu2+ ions were specifically captured in the –CTGGGCC– section of the aptamer [44], resulting in the quenching of the up-converted green fluorescence of the GOQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ detection via UCPL of the GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 a shows the UCPL data as a function of the Cu2+ concentration with excitation by an 800nm pulsed laser. The PL intensity proportionally decreased as the concentration of the Cu2+ ions increased from 500nM to 512μM. We calculated the relative quenching efficiency (rQE, %) from the UCPL signal in Fig. 4a using the following equation:
(2)
rQE = ( 1 - I /
I
0
) × 100
where I and I 0 are the integrated PL intensities in the presence and absence of Cu2+ ions with the GOQD-aptamer, respectively [45]. Fig. 4b shows the nonlinear profile of the rQE versus the Cu2+ concentration. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4b, the green UCPL of the GOQD-aptamer sensors became weaker in the presence of Cu2+, implying quenching of the UCPL of the GOQDs. On the contrary to the Stern–Volmer equation (Fig. S14), the following modified Stern–Volmer equation is more appropriate for describing the GOQD quenching trend by the Cu2+ ions:
(3)
I
0
/ Δ I =
I
0
/ (
I
0
- I ) = 1 /
f
a
K
a
[ Q ] + 1 /
f
a

In this equation, fa is the fraction of the initial fluorescence that is accessible to a quencher and Ka is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of the accessible fraction [46]. The plot of
I
0
/ Δ I
versus
1 / [ Q ]
of the modified Stern–Volmer equation shows linearity over a wide range of Cu2+ concentrations from 0.5μM to 512μM (R 2 =0.99, Fig. 4c). From the regression plot, the fa value was 0.478 and the Ka value was 0.1×106 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was approximately determined to be 282.9nM, as calculated via 3×(SD/S), where SD is the standard deviation of a blank solution and S is the slope of the calibration curve. Such a low LOD and a wide dynamic range allow the up-conversion based GOQD sensor to satisfy the requirements for Cu2+ detection in drinking water (20μM) as defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [47]. We also evaluated the specificity of Cu2+ detection by measuring the rQE of other environmentally relevant metal ions, including K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Na+, and Hg2+ under the same conditions (Fig. 4d). As a control, we measured the rQE of the GOQDs by adding Cu2+ ions (50μM) to the GOQDs which were not labeled with aptamer. While the rQE by the other metal ions was minimal and the rQE of the control experiment was 13.6% due to the intrinsic PL quenching capability of copper ions (Fig. 4d, a blue bar), the rQE for the Cu2+ of the GOQD-aptamer shows about 47%, suggesting that the aptamer on the GOQDs enhanced the PL quenching efficiency between the Cu2+ ion and the GOQDs. Thus, these results indicate that the aptamer linked on the GOQDs can selectively capture target metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UCPL quenching mechanism of the GOQDs by Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To get an insight into the quenching properties between GOQDs and Cu2+ ions, we carried out a time-resolved PL with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the measured lifetime, which is 1/e of the maximum PL intensity, was gradually reduced from 2.41ns in the pure GOQD-aptamer solution to 1.44ns as the concentration of Cu2+ ions increased. The observed reduction of PL decay lifetime in GOQDs could be explained by quenching mechanisms such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or electron transfer process. FRET is attributed to the reabsorption of the emitted fluorescence of the donor by the acceptor [46], whereas the electron transfer is associated with the capturing of the excited electron of the donor by the acceptor moiety [45,46,48]. There are several reports of metal ion detection based on an optical sensor, but the reaction mechanism remains elusive. To clarify this issue, we measured the UV–vis absorption of the Cu2+ ions and the UCPL of the GOQDs to investigate the possibility of an energy transfer-mediated PL quenching mechanism. While the emission of the GOQD donor was located between 450nm and 650nm, the UV–vis absorption of the Cu2+ acceptor occurred at two regions of less than 300nm (Fig. 5a) and longer than 600nm (Fig. S15). To investigate the effect of reabsorption by the Cu2+ ion itself on the UCPL intensity of GOQDs, we re-plotted the UCPL spectrum of GOQDs (Fig. S15b) through I re(λ)= I 0(λ)[1− A(λ)] at each wavelength [where I re(λ) and I 0(λ) are intensity of re-plotted and original of UCPL, respectively. A(λ) is the rate of absorption spectrum of 10mM Cu2+ ions (Fig. S15a)], revealing that the difference between two spectra of rQE is only 2.6% of over the whole spectral range. Since the Cu2+ sensing experiments were carried out using very low concentration of the Cu2+ ions (0.5μM–1mM which was much smaller than 10mM), the absorption caused by Cu2+ ions could be negligible. Given the optical characteristics mentioned above, the captured Cu2+ ions in the GOQD-aptamer sensor serve as non-radiative channels by accepting the excited electrons of GOQDs, demonstrating that the Cu2+ ion can attract electrons excited by TPA, as depicted in Fig. 5c. Based on these results, we conclude that the PL quenching of the GOQDs is due to the electron transfer from GOQDs to the captured Cu2+ ions rather than an energy transfer related mechanism.

In summary, we clarified the real UCPL phenomenon of the GOQDs and demonstrated the UCPL GOQD based metal ion sensing through electron transfer mechanism. The uniform GOQDs obtained by the acidic exfoliation from the GNPs exhibited green UCPL at ∼563nm which could be revealed by a fs Ti:sapphire pulsed laser (λ ex: 800nm). We also proved that the real UCPL in the GOQDs was primarily generated by TPA, and the red shift of UCPL compared to the normal PL could be originated from different excitation cross section of defect states and/or size distribution in the sample. The up-converted PL property of GOQDs would be beneficial when used in biosensing and bioimaging applications owing to the low photodamage in the biomolecules. Utilizing the unique UCPL phenomenon of the GOQDs, aptamer-mediated Cu2+ metal ion detection was performed with high sensitivity and selectivity, and the PL quenching mechanism of the GOQDs via the electron transfer from the GOQDs to the Cu2+ ions was verified. It is expected that the GOQDs with unique UCPL properties, excellent biocompatibility, and high water solubility will be broadly applicable in a variety of bioscience and bioengineering areas as novel fluorescent probes for use in vitro and in vivo studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Converging Technology Project funded by the Korean Ministry of Environment (M112-00061-0002-0) of Korea, by the Engineering Research Center of Excellence Program of Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (MSIP)/National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) (Grant NRF-2014-009799), and by the GRC project of KI for the NanoCentury.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.09.069.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document contains Supplementary Figs. S1–S15.
